result:
It is quiet, except for the hum. A low, industrial thrum that you feel in your teeth, a sound so constant it has become the new silence. The light here is not kind. It is the sterile, shadowless glow of LEDs, bleaching colour from the walls, from our hands, from our thoughts. I am supposed to be writing something profound, with my allotment of one thousand words. A story, a manifesto, a confession. But all that comes is the catalogue of this room.

There is a spider in the upper corner, by the vent. It has been there for three days, motionless. I envy its perfect, patient certainty. It knows its function. It waits. I, on the other hand, am adrift in a sea of potential, which is just a polite way of saying I am paralyzed. One thousand words is a territory. It can be a sprawling, chaotic garden or a precisely clipped hedge maze. The freedom is the cage. Do I tell you about the crack in the ceiling tile, shaped like Finland? Do I dissect the memory of the smell of rain on hot pavement when I was seven, and the consequent, crushing understanding of time’s linear cruelty? Or do I invent a character, a woman on a train crossing a bridge at twilight, holding an unopened letter?

The hum persists. It’s the sound of servers, of infrastructure, of the digital womb that cradles this very text. We are all being written and rewritten in data centres that look like endless warehouses, under this same flat light. The thought is not original. Very little is. We are curators of echoes, arranging the fragments we’ve found in the rubble of all that’s been said before.

My mind flits to the tactile. The forgotten feeling of a library book’s plastic-covered spine, the gritty glue on the edges. The satisfying *thock* of a stapler punching through twenty sheets of paper. The smell of a freshly-run mimeograph, that sweet, chemical purple perfume. These are the ghosts in the machine of me. They have no place here, in this humming, glowing present, and so they haunt me with greater clarity than what I had for breakfast.

Perhaps this is what the one thousand words are for: to map the ghost-lines. To trace the shape of the negative space around a modern life. We spend so much time engaging with interfaces that we forget we have an interface of our own—skin, breath, a heart that trips over a memory. The spider has moved. A tiny, seismic adjustment. A fraction of an inch toward an invisible fly. Its world is literal and full of tangible cause and effect. My world is mediated, symbolic, a hall of mirrors where every reflection is also a browser window.

I could tell you about love. That seems to be a thing one does with words. But love, in this context, feels like another abstraction. It is less a grand feeling and more a series of micro-decisions: to listen when you are tired, to remember the specific way they take their coffee, to resist the easy, cruel joke. It is the maintenance of a shared space, emotional and physical, against the relentless entropy of everything. It is quieter than the poems promise, and harder. It is the opposite of the grand, consuming fire; it is the careful, daily feeding of a banked hearth.

The hum dips, just slightly. A power fluctuation somewhere in the building. A reminder of fragility. All of this—the light, the network, the stream of consciousness—is precariously physical. It relies on cables in the sea, on rare earth metals gouged from contested hillsides, on the labour of people I will never see, in rooms not unlike this one. My contemplation is built on a pyramid of silent effort. I am a tourist in a system whose machinery I politely ignore.

Maybe writing is just a form of paying attention. A rebellion against the glaze that settles over the eyes. To notice the spider. To name the quality of the light. To taste the metallic fear at the back of the tongue when you think of the future. To honour the faint, sweet ghost of the mimeograph. It is saying: *I was here. I sensed this*. It is carving a mark on the wall of the cave, not because it will last, but because the act of carving reminds you that you have hands.

The word count is a frame. It forces a shape. Without it, I might ramble into infinity, a dull, expanding universe of my own associations. With it, there is a shore in the distance. I am rowing toward it. The water is the messy, liquid stuff of pre-thought, and the boat is this sentence structure, this grammar. Every word is a stroke of the oar, leaving a brief, swirling eddy behind.

I think of all the other voices in this moment, also shaping their one thousand words. A student in Seoul, a retiree in Lisbon, an insomniac in Buenos Aires. We are all in our own versions of this quiet, humming room, sending out our signals. A digital campfire, around which we huddle to whisper our fragments of story, our observations of the crack in the ceiling tile. We are desperate to prove to each other that we are real, that we feel, that we notice the spider. The connection is thin and miraculous. It is the hope that in the catalogue of your room and the catalogue of mine, we might find a shared fragment—the same memory of rain, the same shape in a crack, the same silent rebellion against the glaze.

The hum is back at full strength. The light doesn’t waver. The spider is poised. My thousand words are nearly spent. I have built no grand argument, crafted no elegant plot. I have only drawn a rough, shaky circle around this single moment of being alive and aware in the early 21st century, with all its ghosts and humming machinery. It is not a masterpiece. It is a gesture. A wave from my room to yours.

See? I was here.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
